meaning: not much, not really, not particularly, not so
lesson:
あまり almost always appears with a negative. あまり好きじゃない (I don't really like it). あまり食べなかった (I didn't eat much). あまり分からない (I don't really understand). The pattern is simple: あまり + negative = "not very" / "not much."

What makes it useful is the softness. 好きじゃない alone is a flat "I don't like it." Add あまり and it becomes "I don't particularly like it" — politely lukewarm rather than bluntly negative. Japanese conversation relies heavily on this kind of hedging. あまり gives you a way to express mild negation without sounding harsh.

There's a rarer positive usage that means "too much" or "excessively": あまりの暑さに (because of the excessive heat). あまりにもひどい (too terrible). In this sense, the word has a completely different flavor — not mild but extreme. You'll encounter it in writing and dramatic speech, but the negative-pairing usage is what you'll need daily.

写 (copy, reproduce) + 真 (truth, reality). A copy of truth. A reproduction of reality. When this compound was coined in the 19th century, photography was literal magic — a machine that could copy the real world. The name captures that wonder: not a painting, not an interpretation, but truth itself, reproduced.

写真を撮る (to take a photo) uses 撮る (とる), the specific "take" for photography and film. 写真を見せて (show me the photo). In the smartphone era, 写真 is everywhere: 写真撮ってもいいですか (may I take a photo?) is an essential phrase for travel in Japan.

The kanji 真 deserves a closer look. Its original meaning is "genuine, real" — it shows up in 真実 (しんじつ, truth), 真面目 (まじめ, serious/earnest), and 真ん中 (まんなか, the very center). Whenever you see 真 in a word, it's adding emphasis on authenticity — the real deal.

繰 (reel, wind thread) + 返す (to return). Winding the thread back and doing it again. The image of a loom shuttle going back and forth — the same motion, over and over. 同じミスを繰り返す (repeat the same mistake). 歴史は繰り返す (history repeats itself).

The word often carries a note of frustration or inevitability. When something is 繰り返される (repeated), it's usually not a celebration — it's a complaint about things not improving, or an observation about cycles. 何度も繰り返した (did it over and over) has a dogged, weary quality. Compare with もう一回 (one more time), which is neutral, or また (again), which is lighter.

That said, it's perfectly functional in neutral contexts: もう一度繰り返してください (please repeat that one more time) is a polite request in class or conversation. The nuance depends on context, not the word itself.

Particles: 繰り返す takes を — the thing repeated is the direct object. 同じ言葉を繰り返す (repeat the same words).

Potential が繰り返せる: が — what can be repeated. 成功が繰り返せるとは限らない (you can't necessarily repeat success).

Passive に繰り返される: に — used when something keeps being repeated (often impersonal). 同じ過ちが繰り返されている (the same mistake keeps being repeated).

Causative に…を繰り返させる: に marks who you make repeat, を marks what. 生徒にフレーズを繰り返させる (make students repeat the phrase).

五段 Godan他動詞
Dictionaryくりかえすを
Negativeくりかえさないを
Politeくりかえしますを
Polite negくりかえしませんを
Pastくりかえしたを
Polite pastくりかえしましたを
て-formくりかえしてを
Volitionalくりかえそうを
Polite volくりかえしましょうを
Passiveくりかえされるに
Causativeくりかえさせるに／を
Caus-passくりかえさせられるに
Potentialくりかえせるが
Imperativeくりかえせを
Imp なさいくりかえしなさいを
Cond ～ばくりかえせばを
Cond ～たらくりかえしたらを

種 (seed, species) + 類 (kind, type, class). Seeds sorted by type. 種 originally depicted grains of rice being planted — each variety a different seed. 類 has the rice radical too, suggesting different grains being classified. Two kanji about sorting things into groups.

何種類？ (how many types?) is the essential question. いろいろな種類がある (there are various types). 三種類の魚 (three types of fish). The word works as a counter-like noun — you put a number in front of it to specify how many kinds.

種類 vs タイプ: The loanword タイプ is used for personal preferences and personalities — 彼は私のタイプじゃない (he's not my type). 種類 is for actual categorization of things — species, varieties, classifications. You wouldn't say 彼は私の種類じゃない. Different domains.

意 (mind, intention) + 見 (see, view). Your mind's view — what you see when you look at an issue from inside your own head. An opinion.

意見を言う (state one's opinion). 意見が違う (our opinions differ). ご意見をお聞かせください (please let us hear your opinion — formal). The word is neutral in register and fits everywhere from casual discussion to business meetings.

As a する-noun, 意見する means "to give someone a piece of your mind" — to admonish or criticize. This is a different flavor from simply "having" an opinion: 部下に意見する (admonish a subordinate). The する-verb usage carries authority — you're not sharing your view, you're correcting someone. It's mildly old-fashioned but still appears in formal contexts.

新 (new) + 聞 (hear, listen). New things heard — news. The same etymological logic as English "newspaper" (new + paper) but from the ear rather than the page. Chinese uses the same compound, and it was borrowed into Japanese during the Meiji era when newspapers arrived as a Western import.

聞 here uses its on'yomi ぶん, not the kun'yomi きく you know from the verb. This is the compound reading — the formal, Chinese-origin pronunciation. 聞 in this sense means "things heard, reports" — it lives in 新聞社 (しんぶんしゃ, newspaper company) and 新聞記者 (しんぶんきしゃ, newspaper reporter).

新聞を読む (read the newspaper). 新聞に載っていた (it was in the newspaper). 朝刊 (ちょうかん, morning edition) and 夕刊 (ゆうかん, evening edition) are the two daily deliveries in Japan's still-robust newspaper culture. Physical newspaper delivery to homes remains far more common in Japan than in most Western countries.

meaning: sentence(s), writing (in general), any written text
lesson:
文 (sentence, writing, pattern) + 章 (chapter, badge, section). Writing organized into sections — a piece of text, whether it's a single sentence, a paragraph, an essay, or a full document. 文章 is the go-to word when you're talking about written text as a body of work.

文 vs 文章: 文 (ぶん) alone means a single sentence. 文章 means a connected piece of writing — multiple sentences forming something coherent. この文は長い (this sentence is long). この文章は分かりやすい (this writing is easy to understand). The distinction matters when talking about language study: grammar drills deal in 文; reading comprehension deals in 文章.

文章を書く (write a text). 文章力 (ぶんしょうりょく, writing ability). 文章が上手 (good at writing). The word shows up often in school and work contexts — Japanese education places heavy emphasis on 文章 composition.

目 (eye) + 立つ (to stand). Something that stands up to the eye — it catches your attention, it's conspicuous. The compound is beautifully literal: the thing rises from the background and your eyes lock onto it.

The word is neutral — standing out can be good or bad. 目立つ色 (a conspicuous color). 彼女はクラスで目立つ (she stands out in class) — could be talent, beauty, or disruptive behavior. ミスが目立つ (mistakes stand out / errors are noticeable) — that's bad. 目立たないようにする (try not to stand out) — sometimes the Japanese priority.

That last usage touches on something cultural. Japan has the famous proverb 出る杭は打たれる (the nail that sticks out gets hammered down). 目立つ is that nail. Depending on the context, conspicuousness is admired or avoided — and the word itself carries both possibilities with equal ease.

Particles: 目立つ takes が — the conspicuous thing is the subject. 傷が目立つ (the scratch is noticeable). No direct object — this is an intransitive verb.

Passive: rare, since the verb is intransitive. Indirect passive is possible but uncommon.

Causative を目立たせる: making something stand out. ポイントを目立たせる (make the key point stand out). This causative form is actually quite common in design and presentation contexts.

五段 Godan自動詞
Dictionaryめだつが
Negativeめだたないが
Politeめだちますが
Polite negめだちませんが
Pastめだったが
Polite pastめだちましたが
て-formめだってが
Volitionalめだとうが
Polite volめだちましょうが
Passive
Causativeめだたせるを
Caus-passめだたせられるに
Potentialめだてるが
Imperativeめだてが
Imp なさいめだちなさいが
Cond ～ばめだてばが
Cond ～たらめだったらが

相 (mutual, together) + 手 (hand, person). The hand on the other side — the person facing you. But 相手 is deliberately vague about the relationship. It could be your opponent, your partner, your conversation counterpart, or simply "the other person involved." The word refuses to specify whether the relationship is friendly or hostile.

相手チーム (the opposing team). 結婚相手 (marriage partner). 話し相手 (someone to talk to — a conversation partner). 相手にしない (to ignore someone — literally, not make them your counterpart). Same word, wildly different contexts.

The real utility of 相手 is that it lets you talk about "the other person" without naming them or specifying the dynamic. English doesn't have a clean equivalent — we'd say "opponent" in sports, "partner" in business, "the other person" in dating. Japanese just uses 相手 for all of them and lets context do the work.

病 (illness) + 院 (institution, hall). An illness institution. The kanji 病 has 疒 (the sickness radical — a person collapsed on a bed) with 丙 inside contributing the sound. That sickness radical 疒 appears in every disease-related kanji: 痛 (pain), 疲 (fatigue), 症 (symptom). Spot the little bed and you know someone's unwell.

In Japan, 病院 covers both hospitals and what English speakers would call "clinics" or "doctor's offices." There's no strong linguistic distinction between a huge medical center and a small neighborhood practice — both get called 病院 in casual speech. For precision, a large hospital is 総合病院 (そうごうびょういん, general hospital) and a small clinic is クリニック or 医院 (いいん).

病院に行く (go to the hospital) doesn't carry the alarming connotation it does in English. In Japanese, it just means "go see a doctor" — for a cold, a checkup, anything.

Four words pronounced あつい. Four different kanji. This is a minefield. 厚い = thick (a thick book, thick fabric). 熱い = hot to touch (hot coffee). 暑い = hot weather (a summer day). 篤い = devoted, serious (rare, literary). In speech they're identical. In writing, context and kanji sort them out.

厚い specifically is about physical thickness or metaphorical depth. 厚い本 (a thick book). 厚い雲 (thick clouds). 面の皮が厚い (thick-skinned — shameless). 厚いもてなし (warm/generous hospitality — "thick" welcome). The metaphorical uses lean toward abundance and generosity.

Its antonym is 薄い (うすい, thin), which you'll see soon. Keep them as a pair: 厚い / 薄い, thick / thin.

Dictionaryあつい
Negativeあつくない
Pastあつかった
Past negあつくなかった
Adverbあつく
て-formあつくて

The kanji 忙 is 忄 (heart radical) + 亡 (lost, dead, gone). A lost heart. When you're busy, your heart — your presence of mind — goes missing. You're so occupied that the essential "you" has checked out. It's a remarkably honest etymology for what busyness actually feels like.

最近忙しい (I've been busy lately) might be the most frequently uttered sentence in Japanese working life. The word does double duty: it describes both the objective state (having many things to do) and the subjective feeling (being overwhelmed). 忙しくて行けない (I'm too busy to go) is a perfectly acceptable excuse for declining invitations — perhaps the safest one, since busyness in Japan is quasi-respectable.

Sound anchor: i-so-ga-shii has four syllables that tumble out in a rush, mirroring the feeling. The long しい ending makes it whiny, almost like a complaint. Which it often is.

Dictionaryいそがしい
Negativeいそがしくない
Pastいそがしかった
Past negいそがしくなかった
Adverbいそがしく
て-formいそがしくて

薄い is the opposite of 厚い for physical thickness, but it does much more. It covers four English adjectives in one: thin (paper, walls), weak (tea, flavor), light (color, makeup), and pale (skin, hope). The common thread: not enough substance. Something that should be denser, stronger, or deeper — but isn't.

薄い壁 (thin walls). コーヒーが薄い (the coffee is weak). 薄い色 (a pale/light color). 印象が薄い (the impression is faint — unmemorable). Each usage maps to a different English word, but Japanese sees them all as the same quality: insufficient density.

The kanji 薄 has the grass radical (艹) on top — suggesting something spread thin, like grass covering a wide area without much depth. The component 溥 underneath contributes both sound and meaning (to spread thinly). A useful mnemonic: grass is wide but never thick.

Dictionaryうすい
Negativeうすくない
Pastうすかった
Past negうすくなかった
Adverbうすく
て-formうすくて

Three vertical strokes. That's the whole kanji. 川 is a picture of water flowing between two banks — the oldest, most transparent pictograph still in daily use. Oracle bone script from 3,000+ years ago shows three wavy vertical lines; modern 川 just straightened them out. Look at it: the left and right lines are the riverbanks. The middle is the current.

川 vs 河: both mean "river," but 川 (かわ) is the native Japanese word for any river, while 河 (usually かわ or が) shows up in formal or literary contexts and in the names of specifically large rivers. In practice, 川 is the one you'll use. It appears in countless place names — 多摩川 (Tama River), 川崎 (Kawasaki — "river cape"), 石川 (Ishikawa — "stone river").

The word doubles as a metaphor for time and inevitability: 時の流れ (the flow of time) draws on the same image. A river can't flow backward. In haiku and Japanese poetry, 川 is never just water — it's impermanence.

暗い covers physical darkness and emotional gloom in one word. A dark room and a dark personality use the same adjective. 部屋が暗い (the room is dark). 暗い顔 (a gloomy face). 暗い話 (a depressing story). The physical and emotional meanings bleed into each other effortlessly — darkness is sadness, in any language.

Its opposite is 明るい (あかるい, bright/cheerful). The pair 暗い / 明るい maps onto English dark/bright for both rooms and moods.

The kanji 暗 is 日 (sun) + 音 (sound). Sun plus sound seems odd for "dark," but the 音 component is there for the sound value, not the meaning. A different theory: the sun is hidden, and all you have left is sound — you're in the dark, navigating by ear. Whether that's the real etymology or a convenient mnemonic, it sticks. When you can't see, you listen.

There's also a usage meaning "unfamiliar with": その分野には暗い (I'm in the dark about that field — I don't know much about it). This mirrors the English idiom perfectly.

Dictionaryくらい
Negativeくらくない
Pastくらかった
Past negくらくなかった
Adverbくらく
て-formくらくて

From English "class," but narrower. クラス in Japanese primarily means a homeroom class — the group of students you're assigned to, who take all their classes together and share a 教室 (classroom). In Japanese schools, it's the students who move between rooms for different subjects (in middle and high school) or stay put while teachers rotate in (elementary). Your クラス is your social unit for the year.

クラスメート (classmate). クラスの雰囲気 (the atmosphere of the class). 同じクラスになった (we ended up in the same class). In Japanese school life, being in the same クラス as your friend is a big deal — it determines your daily social world for an entire year.

Outside schools, クラス can mean "class" in the sense of grade or rank: ビジネスクラス (business class), ファーストクラス (first class). But for a course or lesson, Japanese uses 授業 (じゅぎょう) or レッスン, not クラス.

The kanji 黒 has fire (灬, the four-dot fire radical at the bottom) and a blocked-off space above it. The original meaning: the color left by fire — soot, char, the blackness that flames leave behind. Every other color kanji in Japanese has a different origin, but 黒 is born from burning.

黒い猫 (a black cat). 黒い服 (black clothes). 黒い雲 (dark clouds). The adjective form 黒い goes directly before nouns. The noun form 黒 (くろ) is the color itself — used when referring to black as a concept or choice: 黒にする (I'll go with black).

In figurative usage, 黒 means guilty or shady — the opposite of 白 (innocent, clean). あの人は黒だ (that person is guilty / shady). 黒幕 (くろまく, the person pulling strings behind the scenes — literally "black curtain"). Japanese noir, built right into the language.

Dictionaryくろい
Negativeくろくない
Pastくろかった
Past negくろくなかった
Adverbくろく
て-formくろくて

From English "bus," itself shortened from Latin "omnibus" (for everyone). The journey from Latin to English to Japanese stripped it down to two syllables: ba-su. Japan's bus system is extensive and reliable, especially in areas where trains don't reach.

バスに乗る (ride the bus — に marks what you board). バスを降りる (get off the bus — を marks what you step out of). バス停 (ばすてい, bus stop). バスが来た (the bus is here). The particle switch between に (boarding) and を (alighting) trips up learners — remember: に is where you enter, を is what you leave.

Watch out: バス can also mean "bass" (as in bass guitar or a bass voice), and in older usage, "bath" (from English "bath" — though 風呂 is far more common for bathing). Context makes the distinction instant, but don't be startled if you see バス in a music context.

青い is the color that broke English speakers' brains. It means "blue" — sky, ocean, blue jeans. But it also means "green" — traffic lights, unripe fruit, fresh vegetables. The 青 traffic signal in Japan is officially called 青 even though it looks green. The 青 apple is unripe even though unripe apples are green. How?

Old Japanese had fewer color categories than modern English. 青 originally covered the entire blue-green spectrum — everything from deep navy to fresh leaf green. Over time, 緑 (みどり) split off to handle green specifically, but the old 青 territory never fully retreated. Traffic lights, green apples, and lush foliage kept their 青 label. It's not that Japanese people can't see the difference between blue and green — it's that the language drew the color boundary in a different place than English did.

青い空 (blue sky). 顔が青い (pale-faced — drained of color, like turning blue/white). 青春 (せいしゅん, youth — literally "blue spring," the green/fresh season of life). That last compound captures the 青 worldview perfectly: young, raw, unripe, full of potential. Not quite a color. More like a stage of being.

Dictionaryあおい
Negativeあおくない
Pastあおかった
Past negあおくなかった
Adverbあおく
て-formあおくて

The kanji 薬 has the grass radical 艹 on top — because medicine came from plants. Underneath sits 楽, which means "comfortable" or "pleasant." Grass that makes you comfortable: medicine. The etymology is so clean it barely needs explaining, and that's exactly what makes it stick.

くすり covers both "medicine" and "drug" with no distinction. 薬を飲む (take medicine — literally "drink" it, even pills). 薬が効く (the medicine works/takes effect). 薬屋 (くすりや, pharmacy/drugstore). The word is neutral — context tells you whether it's Tylenol or something illicit.

Notice the verb pairing: you 飲む medicine in Japanese, not "eat" it and not "take" it. Even a cream you rub on your skin gets described differently — 薬を塗る (ぬる, to apply/spread). But anything that goes in your mouth is 飲む, regardless of whether it's liquid or solid.

Sound first: sa-tou sounds almost exactly like the English name "Sato" — one of the most common Japanese surnames. If you know anyone named Sato, picture them made of sugar. Ridiculous, but that's the point.

The kanji tell a material story. 砂 means sand — granular, dry, pourable. 糖 means sugar as a substance (the chemistry kind). Together: sand-sugar, granulated sweetness. 糖 has the rice radical 米 on the left, because sugar was historically processed from grain starches in East Asia before cane sugar arrived. The right side 唐 (Tang dynasty China) provides the sound and a hint at the trade route that brought refined sugar techniques eastward.

砂糖 is specifically granulated sugar — the white stuff. For the broader concept of sweetness or sugar as a nutrient, Japanese uses 糖 alone in compounds: 糖分 (とうぶん, sugar content), 血糖 (けっとう, blood sugar).

休み is the noun form of 休む (to rest), and it covers an enormous range of English words: a five-minute break, a day off, a national holiday, summer vacation. 今日は休みです could mean "I have the day off" or "we're closed today" or "it's a holiday." The word doesn't care about the scale — if activity stops, that's 休み.

This flexibility matters practically. 夏休み (なつやすみ, summer break). 昼休み (ひるやすみ, lunch break). お休み (おやすみ, goodnight — literally "honorable rest"). That last one is worth noting: お休みなさい is the standard way to say goodnight, and it's built right from this word. You're wishing someone a good rest.

The kanji 休 is a person (亻) leaning against a tree (木). A worker resting in the shade. It's one of those kanji pictures that actually works as a mnemonic without any stretching.

Three-kanji compound, and each piece does clear work. 郵 means postal/mail. 便 means convenience or delivery — the same 便 in 便利 (べんり, convenient). 局 means bureau or office — a government or institutional location. So: mail-delivery-bureau. Bureaucratic precision in three characters.

局 is the useful piece to pull out. It shows up in any institutional office: 薬局 (やっきょく, pharmacy), 放送局 (ほうそうきょく, broadcasting station), 電話局 (でんわきょく, telephone office). When you see 〜局, think "official place where X happens."

Japan's post offices double as banks — you can open a savings account, send money, buy insurance, all at the 郵便局. The postal banking system (ゆうちょ銀行) is one of the largest financial institutions in the world. So 郵便局 in daily Japanese life is closer to "post office slash bank" than the stripped-down stamp-and-package operation most Westerners picture.

住 (to live, reside) + 所 (place) = the place where you live. Your address. Clean logic, easy to remember.

But here's what makes Japanese addresses disorienting for Westerners: they don't work like street addresses. There are no street names in most of Japan. Instead, addresses drill down by area: prefecture → city → ward → district → block → building number. 東京都渋谷区神宮前1-2-3 doesn't name a street at all — it names nested zones, narrowing from broad to specific. Your 住所 is a set of coordinates in a grid of administrative boundaries, not a position along a named road.

This means that when someone asks 住所を教えてください (please tell me your address), the answer is a chain of numbers and area names that might not help you navigate at all without a map. That's why Japanese people so commonly draw little maps when giving directions — the 住所 tells the post office where to deliver, but it doesn't tell a visitor how to walk there.

こちら is the polite version of こっち — both mean "this way" or "here" (my side). You already know the ko-so-a-do system from これ・それ・あれ・どれ. This is the same system in a different outfit: こちら・そちら・あちら・どちら. The 〜ちら series adds politeness and a sense of direction — not just "this thing" but "this side, this direction, over here."

In practice, こちら does double duty as a polite way to introduce someone or present yourself. A receptionist gesturing toward a room: こちらへどうぞ (this way, please). Introducing a colleague: こちらは田中さんです (this is Mr. Tanaka). On the phone, identifying yourself: こちらは山田です (this is Yamada — literally "this side is Yamada").

The pattern is tidy: こっち for friends, こちら for everyone else. Same meaning, different register. You'll hear こちら constantly in shops, restaurants, and offices — it's the default polite spatial word.

財 means wealth (there's 貝, the shell-money radical, on the right). 布 means cloth. A wealth-cloth — a piece of fabric you keep your money in. Wallets were literally pouches once, and the kanji remembers that.

さいふ is gender-neutral in Japanese — it covers wallets, billfolds, coin purses, whatever holds your money. 財布を忘れた (I forgot my wallet) is one of those sentences you'll unfortunately need. 財布を落とした (I dropped/lost my wallet) is the other one.

Japan is still heavily cash-based compared to most developed countries, which makes the 財布 a more central daily object than it might be elsewhere. Losing yours is a genuine crisis. The good news: Japan's lost-and-found culture is legendary. A wallet dropped on a Tokyo street has a remarkably high chance of being turned in intact, cash and all.

Straight from English: "passport" → パスポート. The katakana maps the English sounds as faithfully as the Japanese phonetic system allows — pa-su-po-o-to. If you can say "passport" slowly, you can say this word.

Loanwords like this are freebies for English speakers — you get hundreds of vocabulary words essentially for free. The catch is pronunciation. English speakers tend to say these words with English rhythm and stress, which makes them unintelligible to Japanese listeners. The trick: flatten everything. No stress on any syllable. Each mora gets equal time. PA-SU-PO-O-TO, five even beats. Practice that flatness and every katakana loanword in your vocabulary suddenly becomes usable.

The Japanese passport, incidentally, is consistently ranked among the most powerful in the world for visa-free travel. パスポートを見せてください (please show your passport) — a sentence you'll hear at every border.

Both kanji have the tree radical 木 — because chairs were made of wood. 椅 specifically means chair (you'll almost never see it outside this word). 子 here is a diminutive suffix, not "child" — it softens the word the way "-let" does in English (booklet, droplet). A little wooden sitting-thing.

Traditional Japanese rooms don't have chairs. You sit on the floor, on a 座布団 (ざぶとん, floor cushion), or in 正座 (せいざ, formal kneeling position). Chairs are fundamentally Western furniture that became common in Japan only during modernization. The word 椅子 itself came from Chinese, but the object it describes was long associated with foreign-style rooms. Today chairs are everywhere, of course — but the older floor-sitting culture persists in tatami rooms, traditional restaurants, and tea ceremony.

椅子に座る (sit in/on a chair). Note the particle: に, not の上に. You sit "at" a chair, not "on top of" it, in Japanese spatial logic.

かわいい is arguably the most culturally loaded adjective in modern Japanese. It means "cute" but it's applied to an astonishing range of things — babies, puppies, fashion, handwriting, behavior, food presentation, elderly people, grown men, inanimate objects. Calling something かわいい is one of the most common positive reactions in Japanese social life. It's not just a description; it's a mode of approval.

The word originally meant something darker. In classical Japanese, かわいい (written 可哀想 or derived from かわいそう) meant "pitiable" — something so small and helpless it stirred your sympathy. Over centuries, that protective tenderness evolved into affection, and affection into "cute." The vulnerability at the word's core hasn't fully disappeared — かわいい things tend to be small, soft, round, harmless. The aesthetic values the unthreatening.

The kanji 可愛い is rarely written — かわいい in hiragana is standard. When you do see the kanji, 可 means "possible" and 愛 means "love" — "possible to love," "lovable." Which is, honestly, a pretty good definition of cute.

Dictionaryかわいい
Negativeかわいくない
Pastかわいかった
Past negかわいくなかった
Adverbかわいく
て-formかわいくて

Japanese has two words for grandfather, and the choice depends on whose grandfather you're talking about. おじいさん (with the honorific お〜さん wrapper) is for someone else's grandfather, or for addressing your own grandfather directly. When talking about your own grandfather to others, you'd use the humble 祖父 (そふ) instead. This in-group/out-group distinction runs through all Japanese family terms.

おじいさん also means "old man" or "elderly gentleman" in general — you can call any older man おじいさん the way English might use "grandpa" loosely. But be careful: calling someone おじいさん when they don't consider themselves old is a quick way to offend. The boundary between おじさん (middle-aged man) and おじいさん (old man) is one people defend fiercely.

Sound trap: おじいさん (grandfather, long じい) vs. おじさん (uncle/middle-aged man, short じ). That one extra beat of vowel length is the entire difference between "grandpa" and "mister." Stretch the wrong one and you've aged someone thirty years.

切手 literally means "cut-hand" — which sounds bizarre until you learn the history. In old Japan, 切手 was a receipt or coupon that you "cut off" (切る) from a larger document as proof of payment. The postal system adopted the word for its prepaid adhesive stamps, and the original meaning faded. The "hand" (手) here means document or certificate, not a body part — the same usage as in 手紙 (てがみ, letter).

Japan takes stamp collecting seriously. The postal service regularly issues special commemorative 切手 with artwork, seasonal themes, anime characters, and local landmarks. 切手を貼る (はる, to stick/affix a stamp) — note the verb: 貼る, the same one used for stickers and wallpaper. You don't "put" a stamp, you "stick" it.

80円切手をください (an 80-yen stamp, please) is the sentence you'll use at the post office. The price goes right before 切手 as a modifier.

Japan has a dedicated vocabulary for temperature that's more precise than English. 涼しい fills one very specific slot: the pleasant cool of a breeze on a warm day, the first relief of autumn air, the shade under a tree in summer. It's nice cool — never unpleasant. If the cool becomes uncomfortable, you've crossed into 寒い (さむい, cold) territory.

The temperature words form a neat chain: 暑い (hot weather) → 涼しい (pleasantly cool) → 寒い (cold weather). And for things you touch: 熱い (hot to touch) → 冷たい (cold to touch). 涼しい only works for ambient temperature and air — you'd never describe an ice cube or a cold drink as 涼しい.

The kanji 涼 has the water radical 氵 on the left — coolness associated with water, evaporation, relief. 涼しい風 (a cool breeze) is one of those phrases that practically carries a physical sensation.

Dictionaryすずしい
Negativeすずしくない
Pastすずしかった
Past negすずしくなかった
Adverbすずしく
て-formすずしくて

いくつ is いく (how many) + つ (the native Japanese counter). It asks "how many?" when you're counting generic things with the つ system (一つ、二つ、三つ…). りんごはいくつ？ (how many apples?). Simple enough.

The surprise is the second meaning: "how old are you?" おいくつですか？ is a standard (and polite, with the お) way to ask someone's age. Why? Because the old way of counting years used the つ counter — 一つ、二つ… — for ages up to about nine. The practice faded, but the question word stuck. So おいくつですか is literally "how many (years)?" fossilized into a polite age question.

いくつ vs いくら: Both start with いく (how many/much), but いくつ counts discrete things and いくら asks about price or amount. いくつ？ = how many? / how old? いくら？ = how much (money)? Don't mix them up — asking いくら about someone's age sounds like you're pricing them.

From French via English: menu → メニュー. The ニュー part trips up English speakers who want to say "men-oo" — in Japanese it's closer to me-nyu-u, three clean beats with that distinctly Japanese にゅ combination sound.

In a Japanese restaurant, メニューをください gets you the menu. But メニュー extends beyond food — it's used for any list of options: a computer menu, a spa menu of treatments, a set of choices. 今日のメニュー can mean "today's offerings" in any context.

At many Japanese restaurants, especially chains and casual spots, the メニュー comes with photographs of every dish. This isn't considered low-end — it's standard, even expected. The visual menu culture means you can often just point. これをください (this one, please) while pointing at the picture is a perfectly normal way to order.

電 (lightning/electricity) + 気 (energy/spirit) = electric energy. But in everyday Japanese, 電気 usually just means "the light" — as in, the light in the room. 電気をつける (turn on the light). 電気を消す (turn off the light). When your mom yells about leaving the lights on, she's yelling about 電気.

This double meaning — "electricity" the concept and "the light" the household object — is just something you absorb through context. 電気代 (でんきだい, electricity bill) is clearly about the utility. 電気がついている (the light is on) is clearly about the room light. Nobody gets confused.

電 is worth learning well — it anchors a massive family of modern vocabulary: 電話 (phone), 電車 (train), 電池 (battery), 電子 (electronic). All of them are "lightning-something" — phone is lightning-talk, train is lightning-car. The kanji 電 itself shows rain (雨) over a field with a flash — a thunderstorm captured in ink.

勝つ is intransitive — a fact that surprises English speakers. In English, you "win a game" (transitive). In Japanese, you win at or against: 試合に勝つ (win at the match), 相手に勝つ (win against the opponent). You don't "win" the match as a direct object — the match is the arena where winning happens.

Its partner 負ける (まける, to lose) works the same way: 試合に負ける. These two are a pair you should learn together. 勝つ and 負ける always take に — the game, the opponent, the challenge.

The kanji 勝 has 力 (power) at the bottom right. The victorious one is the one with power. It shows up in compounds like 勝利 (しょうり, victory) and 勝負 (しょうぶ, match/contest — literally "win-lose"). That last compound pairs our word perfectly with its opposite.

Particles: 勝つ takes に — the opponent or contest you win against/at. 彼に勝つ (win against him). The に marks the arena or adversary of victory.

Passive に勝たれる: に marks who beat you. ライバルに勝たれた (my rival won — and I'm the one affected). Carries that adversative passive nuance.

Causative を／に勝たせる: making or letting someone win. を for coercive, に for permissive. 子供に勝たせる (let the kid win).

五段 Godan自動詞
Dictionaryかつに
Negativeかたないに
Politeかちますに
Polite negかちませんに
Pastかったに
Polite pastかちましたに
て-formかってに
Volitionalかとうに
Polite volかちましょうに
Passiveかたれるに
Causativeかたせるを／に
Caus-passかたせられるに
Potentialかてるに
Imperativeかてに
Imp なさいかちなさいに
Cond ～ばかてばに
Cond ～たらかったらに

meaning: to lose (in a match/competition), be defeated
lesson:
負ける is the other half of the 勝つ/負ける pair. Win and lose. Both intransitive, both take に. 試合に負けた (lost the match). 彼女に負けた (lost to her).

But 負ける has a second life that 勝つ doesn't: discounts. When haggling at a market or asking for a deal, 負けてください means "give me a discount" — literally "please lose (some of the price)." The seller "loses" by lowering the price. This usage is casual and only works in certain contexts (flea markets, small shops, some electronics districts), but it's charming and worth knowing.

The kanji 負 shows a person (人 in its bent form) carrying something on their back (貝, the shell). To bear a burden. Losing is carrying the weight of defeat. The same kanji appears in 負担 (ふたん, burden) and 負傷 (ふしょう, injury) — all things you carry unwillingly.

Particles: 負ける takes に — the opponent or contest you lose to/at. 相手に負ける (lose to the opponent). に marks the force that defeated you.

Passive に負けられる: rare, since 負ける is already about receiving an outcome. When used, に marks who lost (affecting you): 味方に負けられると困る (it's a problem if our side loses).

Causative を／に負けさせる: making someone lose. 相手を負けさせる (make the opponent lose / defeat them).

一段 Ichidan自動詞 — pair: 勝つ (かつ)
Dictionaryまけるに

Politeまけますに
Polite negまけませんに
Pastまけたに
Polite pastまけましたに
て-formまけてに

建てる means to build structures — houses, buildings, monuments. Not crafts, not furniture, not sandcastles. This is serious construction. 家を建てる (build a house). ビルを建てる (erect a building).

たてる confusion: Japanese has multiple verbs pronounced たてる, and they all mean slightly different things. 建てる = build structures. 立てる = stand something up, set up (a plan, a sound). 点てる = brew tea (tea ceremony). The kanji sorts them out in writing, but in speech you rely on context. If someone says いえをたてる, it's always 建てる. If they say けいかくをたてる (make a plan), that's 立てる.

The kanji 建 has the radical 廴 (a long stride) at the bottom — the idea of setting something up with forward motion, raising something that wasn't there before. Its intransitive partner is 建つ (たつ, to be built): 新しいビルが建った (a new building was built / went up).

Particles: 建てる takes を — the structure is your direct object. 家を建てる. You can add に for where: 駅の近くに家を建てる (build a house near the station).

Potential が建てられる: が marks what can be built. この土地に家が建てられる (a house can be built on this land).

Passive に建てられる: に marks who built it. 有名な建築家に建てられた (built by a famous architect).

Causative に…を建てさせる: に marks who you make build, を marks the structure. 業者に家を建てさせる (have a contractor build a house).

日 (day) + 記 (record) = a daily record. A diary. The kanji couldn't be more transparent.

Japan has a long literary tradition of diary writing. The 土佐日記 (とさにっき), written around 935 AD, is one of the earliest works of Japanese prose — and it's a travel diary. The 枕草子 (Pillow Book) is essentially a diary. The form has deep cultural prestige that it never quite achieved in the West.

In modern Japan, keeping a 日記 is commonly assigned as school homework — students practice writing by recording what happened each day. 日記をつける (to keep a diary) uses the verb つける (to attach/keep at). 日記を書く (to write a diary entry) works for individual entries. The verbs are different because one describes the ongoing habit and the other the specific act of writing.

売り (selling) + 切れ (cut off, run out) = sold until cut off. Sold out. The 切れ here is the same one in 切れる (to run out, be cut) — the supply line has been severed. Nothing left.

You'll see this word plastered on signs, websites, and vending machine buttons. 売り切れ — two characters that crush your hopes at a bakery, a concert box office, or a limited-edition product launch. チケットは売り切れです (tickets are sold out). もう売り切れた (already sold out) uses the past-tense verb form.

Japan's culture of limited-edition runs and seasonal products means you encounter 売り切れ constantly. A special flavor at a convenience store, a collaboration product, a particular train bento — they come and go fast. Learning to read this word on a sign saves you the disappointment of asking.

お巡りさん breaks down beautifully. 巡り comes from 巡る (まわる, to patrol/go around). Add the honorific お prefix and the friendly さん suffix, and you get "the honorable one who goes around" — the officer on patrol. It's affectionate, approachable, the word a child would use to flag down a cop.

This is the casual, everyday word for police officer. The formal equivalent is 警察官 (けいさつかん), which you'd hear on the news. お巡りさん is what you'd say to a neighbor, in a story, or when teaching kids about who to go to if they're lost.

Japanese police officers really do patrol on foot and by bicycle through neighborhoods — the 交番 (こうばん, police box) system puts small stations throughout residential areas, and officers walk regular routes. The word お巡りさん reflects this reality: the friendly figure making rounds, not a distant authority behind a desk.

Four kanji, perfectly logical. 目覚まし means "eye-awakening" — from 目 (eye) + 覚ます (to wake/awaken). 時計 means clock (時 = time, 計 = measure). An eye-awakening time-measurer. An alarm clock.

The word is long — six syllables, me-za-ma-shi-do-ke-i — which is why Japanese often shortens it to just 目覚まし in casual speech. 目覚ましをかける (set the alarm). 目覚ましが鳴った (the alarm went off — literally "rang").

Note how 時計 becomes どけい here, not とけい. This is 連濁 (rendaku) — the voicing that happens when a word becomes the second element of a compound. The unvoiced と shifts to voiced ど. You've seen this before: 花火 (はなび, not はなひ). It's not consistent — some compounds do it, some don't — but 時計 always voices in compounds.

From English "receipt" — re-shi-i-to. The "c" disappears (it's silent in English too) and the "p" and "t" each get their own vowel, as katakana demands.

In Japanese shops, the cashier will often ask レシートはよろしいですか — which literally means "is the receipt all right?" but actually means "do you want the receipt?" Saying いいです or 大丈夫です means you don't need it. This is a daily micro-interaction you'll have hundreds of times in Japan.

レシート vs 領収書: Both mean receipt, but they're different documents. レシート is the register printout — the cash register tape. 領収書 (りょうしゅうしょ) is a formal, handwritten receipt with the store's stamp, used for business expenses and tax purposes. If you need to expense something, you ask for a 領収書, not a レシート.

From English "tissue" — ti-sshu. The ティ combination is relatively modern in Japanese — older speakers might say チッシュ instead, since ティ didn't exist in traditional katakana. The small ィ marks it as a newer phonetic adaptation.

Pocket tissue packs are a defining feature of Japanese street life. Companies hire people to hand out free ティッシュ at train stations and busy intersections — each pack has an advertisement tucked inside. It's one of the most distinctly Japanese marketing tactics, and it means most people in Japan are carrying tissues at all times. ティッシュをもらう (receive a tissue pack) — a daily occurrence near any major station.

This matters because many public restrooms in Japan, especially older ones, don't provide toilet paper. Having a pocket ティッシュ on you isn't just for sneezing — it's practical infrastructure.

歯 (tooth) + ブラシ (brush, from English) = toothbrush. Half kanji, half katakana — a hybrid word that shows how naturally Japanese absorbs foreign elements. The tooth part is native; the brush part is imported.

ブラシ alone means any kind of brush — hairbrush, paintbrush, scrub brush. Sticking 歯 in front specifies it for teeth. This pattern works broadly: 歯磨き (はみがき, toothpaste — literally "tooth-polishing"), 歯医者 (はいしゃ, dentist — literally "tooth-doctor").

At Japanese hotels, 歯ブラシ is typically provided as a disposable amenity, individually wrapped. The quality is usually minimal — a thin handle, basic bristles. Many travelers bring their own. 歯ブラシを持ってきた？ (did you bring a toothbrush?) — a practical travel question.

meaning: to go down, descend, get off (bus, train, etc), land
lesson:
下りる is downward movement: descending stairs, getting off a train, climbing down a mountain, a plane landing. The kanji 下 (below/down) makes the direction clear. 電車を下りる (get off the train). 山を下りる (descend the mountain).

下りる vs 降りる: These are the same word, おりる, with two possible kanji. 下りる emphasizes physically going downward — descending stairs, coming down from a height. 降りる emphasizes getting off or out of a vehicle. In practice, most people write 降りる for the train/bus sense and 下りる for literal descent, but they're interchangeable and the kana おりる works for both.

The を particle here is worth noting. 下りる is intransitive — you're not acting on the train — but を marks the place of departure, the same way it does with 出る. バスを下りる = leave the bus by going down and out of it.

Particles: 下りる takes を to mark the vehicle or place you exit — バスを下りる. This を isn't a direct object — it marks the departure point. For descending to a place, use に: 一階に下りる (go down to the first floor).

Passive に下りられる: someone's getting off affects you. 途中で下りられると困る (it'd be a problem if they got off midway).

Causative を／に下りさせる: making or letting someone get off. ここで下りさせてください (please let me off here).

The kanji 洗 is water (氵) + ahead/previous (先). Think of it as putting water first — water before everything else, because washing comes before cleanliness. The radical does the heavy lifting: if you see 氵, water is involved.

洗う covers all washing — hands, dishes, clothes, cars, hair. 手を洗う (wash your hands). 車を洗う (wash the car). お皿を洗う (wash the dishes). Unlike English, which might use "do" (do the dishes) or "clean" depending on the object, Japanese keeps it simple: if water and scrubbing are involved, it's 洗う.

Compounds worth knowing: 洗濯 (せんたく, laundry), 洗面所 (せんめんじょ, washroom/sink area), 洗剤 (せんざい, detergent). All built on the same 洗 character, all about cleaning with water.

Particles: 洗う takes を — what you wash is the direct object. 手を洗う, 食器を洗う. You're acting on the thing with water.

Potential が洗える: が — what can be washed becomes the subject. このセーターは洗濯機で洗える (this sweater can be washed in a machine).

Passive に洗われる: に marks who washes. Uncommon but valid: 波に洗われた岩 (rocks washed by waves).

Causative に…を洗わせる: に marks who you make wash, を marks what. 子供に手を洗わせる (make the kid wash their hands).

Borrowed from "part-time" but trimmed to just the first word: パート. In Japanese usage, this specifically means a part-time job — and more specifically, it usually refers to part-time work done by married women, often in retail, food service, or light manufacturing. There's a social dimension English "part-time" doesn't carry.

パート vs アルバイト: Both mean part-time work, but they're aimed at different people. アルバイト (from German Arbeit), often shortened to バイト, is what students and young people do. パート is what homemakers and older adults do. The work might be identical — behind a register, stocking shelves — but the word signals who you are. パートで働いている (I work part-time) implies a married woman supplementing household income. バイトしている implies a student or young person earning spending money.

There's a legal and economic dimension too. Japan's tax system has an income threshold (the so-called "103万円の壁") that affects whether パート workers owe tax, making the distinction between part-time categories practically significant.

氏 (family name/clan) + 名 (given name) = full name. But you'll almost never hear this word in conversation. 氏名 lives on forms, documents, official applications, and bureaucratic instructions. It's the "full legal name" version of asking who you are.

If you fill out any paperwork in Japan — at a hotel, a bank, a government office, a doctor — you'll see 氏名 printed next to a blank line. That's where your full name goes. Sometimes the form separates it into 氏 (family name) and 名 (given name) on separate lines. In Japan, family name comes first — always.

In casual life, you'd use 名前 (なまえ) for "name." 氏名 is strictly for forms and official contexts. Knowing this word is less about conversation and more about literacy — recognizing it when you see it on paper.
